user_goals_summary: Documentation should explain why a system was built the way it was, not just describe what it does. Every element — prose, code, headers, warnings — must earn its place by doing work the other elements can't. The north star is: would this have saved an engineer an hour of confusion?

rubric.name: Design Rationale Over Feature Description
rubric.description: Every architectural fact is motivated by the reasoning behind it. Prose explains why a design choice was made and what tradeoff it accepts, not just what the system does. Without the 'why,' the documentation reads like code comments promoted to prose.

dimensions.label: Each architectural fact is preceded or accompanied by the reasoning behind it
Tradeoffs are stated explicitly rather than left for the reader to infer
Prose reads as explanation of design thinking, not as a feature list or system description
Reader comes away understanding the system's design philosophy, not just its behavior

rubric.name: Operational Warnings Surfaced Early
rubric.description: Real-world footguns and failure modes are placed prominently before the code a reader will copy — not buried in a separate troubleshooting section. Each warning is actionable: it names what goes wrong and what to do about it.

dimensions.label: Known footguns appear before the code block they relate to
Warnings are visually distinct (bold lead-ins, separate paragraphs) — not buried in flowing prose
Warnings address failure modes a practitioner would actually hit, not theoretical risks
Each warning includes what to do about the problem, not just what can go wrong

rubric.name: Minimal Happy-Path Code
rubric.description: Code examples show the single most common usage path in the most compact form possible. No helper functions to unpack, no multi-scenario demonstrations. The prose has already done the explaining; the code only needs to show the shape of the call.

dimensions.label: Code shows one usage path — the most common one — and nothing else
Code is a direct call, not wrapped in function definitions or classes the reader must mentally unpack
Code is tight enough to drop into a codebase without stripping scaffolding
Key data structures or response shapes are visible, e.g. as a single inline comment

rubric.name: Prose-Code Non-Redundancy
rubric.description: Prose and code have distinct, non-overlapping jobs. If the prose explained something, the code doesn't re-explain it in a comment. Redundancy between the two is treated as a signal that the prose didn't finish its job, not that the code needs more annotation.

dimensions.label: Information conveyed in prose is not repeated in code comments
Prose completes its explanatory job before the code block begins
Code comments show data shapes or structural annotations, not usage instructions already covered in prose

rubric.name: Rejected-Alternative Framing
rubric.description: Design choices are explained by naming the common alternative that was not chosen and stating concretely why it falls short. The 'common pattern, but...' structure lets the reader see the reasoning through contrast rather than assertion.

dimensions.label: When explaining a design choice, names the familiar alternative that was not chosen
States concretely why the alternative falls short or creates problems
Uses the rejection to naturally motivate the actual design, creating a logical flow

rubric.name: Intent-Signaling Headers
rubric.description: Headers signal the section's argument or narrative through-line, not just its topic. A reader scanning only the headers should get a sense of what they're walking into — the header is the first sentence of the story, not a file folder label.

dimensions.label: Header hints at the section's argument or through-line, not just its subject matter
Avoids generic labels like 'Getting Started,' 'Overview,' or 'Introduction'
Header creates enough context or curiosity that a scanning reader would stop and engage

coaching_notes: This user's ultimate test is 'would this have saved someone an hour?' — every sentence must pass that bar. They treat redundancy between prose and code as a failure of the prose, not a gap in the code comments. Their natural editing direction is always toward compression and purpose: if an element isn't doing unique work, it should be cut rather than justified.

No helper functions to unpack, no multi-scenario demonstrations, no stray imports or boilerplate. The prose has already done the explaining; the code only needs to show the shape of the call. dimensions.label: Code shows one usage path — the most common one — and nothing else

rubric.name: Intent-Signaling Headers That Tell a Story
rubric.description: Headers signal the section's argument or narrative through-line, not just its topic. A reader scanning only the headers should get a coherent sense of what the document is walking them through — the headers together should read like a table of contents for a tutorial, not a filing system. Generic labels like 'Overview' or 'Introduction' are a signal the author hasn't committed to a point.

dimensions.label: Header hints at the section's argument or through-line, not just its subject matter
Avoids generic labels like 'Getting Started,' 'Overview,' or 'Introduction'
Header creates enough context or curiosity that a scanning reader would stop and engage
Headers read sequentially as a coherent narrative arc, not as isolated topic labels

rubric.name: Respects the Reader's Intelligence Without Abandoning Them
rubric.description: Documentation assumes the reader is a competent engineer who is simply unfamiliar with this specific system. It does not over-explain general programming concepts, but it also does not use minimizing language — words like 'simply,' 'just,' 'obviously,' or 'easy' that make a struggling reader feel at fault rather than helped. Difficulty is acknowledged where it exists. Plain, honest language is preferred over theatrical replacements — the goal is to remove false reassurance, not to heighten stakes.

dimensions.label: Does not use minimizing language ('simply,' 'just,' 'obviously,' 'easy') that trivializes genuine complexity — plain, honest phrasing is the target, not dramatic alternatives
Assumes engineering competence — does not over-explain general concepts unrelated to this system
Acknowledges when something is genuinely complex or counterintuitive rather than glossing over it
Tone is collegial — written as one competent engineer explaining to another, not as instructor to student
Replacements for minimizing language are plain and direct — not dramatic or heightened in register. 'Probably won't notice' is a better fix than 'neither will betray you'

rubric.name: Design Rationale Over Feature Description
rubric.description: Every architectural fact is motivated by the reasoning behind it. Prose explains why a design choice was made and what tradeoff it accepts, not just what the system does. Without the 'why,' the documentation reads like code comments promoted to prose.

Reader comes away understanding the system's design philosophy, not just its behavior

dimensions.label: Information conveyed in prose is not repeated in code comments
Prose completes its explanatory job before the code block begins
Code comments show data shapes or structural annotations, not usage instructions already covered in prose

When real incidents or experience motivated a decision, that evidence adds weight — but presented as institutional knowledge, not as a dated postmortem.

dimensions.label: Each architectural fact is preceded or accompanied by the reasoning behind it

dimensions.label: Information conveyed in prose is not repeated in code comments

coaching_notes: This user treats documentation as a tool for preempting foreseeable questions — if the next engineer will wonder 'why not just do X?', the doc should answer that before they ask. Tone is a hard constraint: plain, honest, engineer-to-engineer. Both minimizing language ('just do X') and dramatic replacements ('neither will betray you') are rejected equally — the target register is matter-of-fact. The user wants docs that feel like living reference material, not artifacts of a specific moment — real incidents add weight but names, dates, and postmortem framing must be stripped.

rubric.name: Minimal Happy-Path Code
rubric.description: Code examples show the single most common usage path in the most compact form possible. No helper functions to unpack, no multi-scenario demonstrations, no stray imports or boilerplate. The prose has already done the explaining; the code only needs to show the shape of the call. Each command or logical unit in a code block carries a short orientation comment so a skimmer knows what it does at a glance — these comments are for navigation, not elaboration.

dimensions.label: Code shows one usage path — the most common one — and nothing else
Code is a direct call, not wrapped in function definitions or classes the reader must mentally unpack
No unnecessary imports, config setup, or boilerplate that isn't central to the point being demonstrated
Code is tight enough to drop into a codebase without stripping scaffolding

Each command or logical unit carries a short orientation comment (2–5 words) so a skimmer knows what it does at a glance — this comment is for navigation, not elaboration. Its content should not duplicate prose; its existence should not be eliminated just because prose covers the same ground. The test is whether a skimmer would know what the line does without reading the prose.

If a code example needs more than a handful of lines, treat that as a smell — a signal the surrounding prose may not have done its job — but do not use line count as a reason to strip orientation comments that serve scannability rather than explanation

dimensions.label: Information conveyed in prose is not repeated in code comments
Prose completes its explanatory job before the code block begins

Code comments provide information not already present in the prose — whether structural, purposive, or contextual. A one-phrase orientation label is not redundancy; it is scannability scaffolding. Non-redundancy means comments should not re-explain the detail prose already covered, not that comments must be omitted whenever prose touches the same topic.

user_goals_summary: Documentation should teach engineers why a system was built the way it was, not just describe what it does. Every architectural fact needs its rationale, rejected alternatives should be surfaced to preempt foreseeable objections, and real-world failure modes must appear before the code a reader will copy. Tone is engineer-to-engineer: honest, plain, and neither condescending nor dramatic.
coaching_notes: This user treats documentation as a tool for preempting foreseeable questions — if the next engineer will wonder 'why not just do X?', the doc should answer that before they ask. Tone is a hard constraint: plain, honest, engineer-to-engineer. Both minimizing language ('just do X') and dramatic replacements ('neither will betray you') are rejected equally — the target register is matter-of-fact. The user wants docs that feel like living reference material, not artifacts of a specific moment — real incidents add weight but names, dates, and postmortem framing must be stripped.

writing_type: internal technical documentation (wiki pages, runbooks, getting-started guides for engineers)
user_goals_summary: Documentation should teach engineers why a system was built the way it was, not just describe what it does. Prose does the heavy lifting — earning conclusions through reasoning chains, naming rejected alternatives, and surfacing real-world footguns — while code stays minimal and copy-pasteable. Claims and conclusions must be precisely bounded to what the evidence actually shows, never broader.

rubric.name: Rejected-Alternative Framing
rubric.description: Design choices are explained by naming the common alternative that was not chosen and stating concretely why it falls short. The 'common pattern, but...' structure lets the reader see the reasoning through contrast rather than assertion. Without the rejected alternative, you're just restating the code in prose form — the rejected path is where the actual learning happens.

dimensions.label: When explaining a design choice, names the familiar alternative the reader is likely already considering
States concretely why the alternative falls short — not just that it was rejected, but what specific failure it permits
Uses the rejection to naturally motivate the actual design, creating a logical flow from 'this doesn't work' to 'so we did this instead'
Does not treat 'we chose X' as a complete explanation — the choice alone teaches nothing without the rejected path
When opening a contrast, the completing thought is fully formed — an incomplete or truncated comparison is worse than no addition at all

rubric.name: Design Rationale Over Feature Description
rubric.description: Every architectural fact is motivated by the reasoning behind it. Prose explains why a design choice was made and what tradeoff it accepts — and tradeoffs must name the specific cost, not just assert that one was accepted. 'That's the tradeoff' is not an explanation; 'that penalizes clients for being idle' is. When real incidents motivated a decision, that evidence adds weight — presented as institutional knowledge, not as a dated postmortem.

dimensions.label: Each architectural fact is preceded or accompanied by the reasoning behind it
Tradeoffs name the concrete thing that would be lost or sacrificed — not just 'this is a tradeoff we accepted' but what specifically the alternative would cost
Prose reads as explanation of design thinking, not as a feature list or system description
When referencing real incidents or experience that motivated a decision, presents them as institutional knowledge — not tied to specific people, dates, or event timelines that date the document

rubric.name: Operational Warnings and Diagnostic Forks Surfaced Early
rubric.description: Real-world footguns and failure modes are placed prominently before the code a reader will copy — not buried in a separate troubleshooting section. When multiple root causes produce the same symptom, the diagnostic fork is presented before any individual conclusion, so the reader follows the right investigation path from the start. Each warning is actionable: it names what goes wrong and what to do about it.

dimensions.label: Known footguns appear before the code block they relate to
Warnings are visually distinct (bold lead-ins, separate paragraphs) — not buried in flowing prose
Warnings address failure modes a practitioner would actually hit, not theoretical risks
Each warning includes what to do about the problem, not just what can go wrong
When multiple root causes could explain the same symptom, all branches are surfaced before the reader reaches any individual conclusion — not appended as an afterthought

rubric.name: Minimal Happy-Path Code
rubric.description: Code examples show the single most common usage path in the most compact form possible. No helper functions to unpack, no multi-scenario demonstrations, no stray imports or boilerplate. The prose has already done the explaining; the code only needs to show the shape of the call. Every command or logical unit carries a short orientation comment so a skimmer knows what they're looking at — this is non-negotiable even when prose covers the same ground.

dimensions.label: Code shows one usage path — the most common one — and nothing else
Code is a direct call, not wrapped in function definitions or classes the reader must mentally unpack
No unnecessary imports, config setup, or boilerplate that isn't central to the point being demonstrated

Each command or logical unit carries a short orientation comment so a skimmer knows what it does at a glance — this exists for navigation, not elaboration, and is required even when prose covers the topic
Runnable commands are not interleaved with inline output annotations — commands must be copy-pasteable as-is, with output or field explanations separated clearly
Prose completes its explanatory job before the code block begins; if the code block grows beyond a handful of lines, that signals the prose may not have finished its job

rubric.name: Precise, Bounded Claims
rubric.description: Conclusions and diagnostic statements claim exactly what the evidence supports — no broader. Documentation does not offer verdicts ('healthy,' 'working as designed') when the data only supports narrower observations ('no single node recorded a violation'). Where a structured diagnostic path has been laid out, prose reflects that structure with direct statements rather than hedges. Gaps between what was observed and what the reader wants to know are named, not papered over.

dimensions.label: Closing or summary statements claim only what the presented data actually shows, not broader inferences
Does not offer 'all clear' verdicts when the observation is narrower than the system-level question being investigated
When a proper diagnostic path has been laid out, uses direct statements rather than hedges like 'almost certainly' or 'probably'
When a gap exists between what was observed and what the reader wants to know, the gap is named explicitly rather than glossed over

rubric.name: Intent-Signaling Headers That Tell a Story
rubric.description: Headers signal the section's argument or narrative through-line, not just its topic. A reader scanning only the headers should get a coherent sense of what the document is walking them through — the headers together should read like a table of contents for a tutorial, not a filing system. Generic labels like 'Overview' or 'Introduction' are a signal the author hasn't committed to a point.

dimensions.label: Header hints at the section's argument or through-line, not just its subject matter
Avoids generic labels like 'Getting Started,' 'Overview,' or 'Introduction'
Header creates enough context or curiosity that a scanning reader would stop and engage
Headers read sequentially as a coherent narrative arc, not as isolated topic labels

rubric.name: Respects the Reader's Intelligence Without Abandoning Them
rubric.description: Documentation assumes the reader is a competent engineer who is simply unfamiliar with this specific system. It does not over-explain general concepts, but it also does not use minimizing language — 'simply,' 'just,' 'obviously' — that makes a struggling reader feel at fault. Plain, honest language is preferred over theatrical replacements. Difficulty is acknowledged where it exists.

dimensions.label: Does not use minimizing language ('simply,' 'just,' 'obviously,' 'easy') that trivializes genuine complexity
Assumes engineering competence — does not over-explain general concepts unrelated to this specific system
Acknowledges when something is genuinely complex or counterintuitive rather than glossing over it
Tone is collegial — written as one competent engineer explaining to another, not as instructor to student
Replacements for minimizing language are plain and direct — not dramatic or heightened in register

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every code example must have a short orientation comment per command, even when prose covers the same topic — the user treats this as a firm rule, not a preference. This user's core instinct is that documentation teaches through reasoning chains, not descriptions; the rejected alternative is where learning happens, and the tradeoff must name its concrete cost. When offering diagnostic conclusions, match the precision of the claim to the precision of the evidence — this user will push back on any language that's broader than what the data shows.

rubric.description: Code examples show the single most common usage path in the most compact form possible. No helper functions to unpack, no multi-scenario demonstrations, no stray imports or boilerplate. The prose has already done the explaining; the code only needs to show the shape of the call. Every command or logical unit carries a short orientation comment so a skimmer knows what they're looking at — this is non-negotiable even when prose covers the same ground.

dimensions.label: Code shows one usage path — the most common one — and nothing else
Code is a direct call, not wrapped in function definitions or classes the reader must mentally unpack
No unnecessary imports, config setup, or boilerplate that isn't central to the point being demonstrated

Each command or logical unit carries a short orientation comment so a skimmer knows what it does at a glance — this exists for navigation, not elaboration, and is required even when prose covers the topic
Runnable commands are not interleaved with inline output annotations — commands must be copy-pasteable as-is, with output or field explanations separated clearly
Prose completes its explanatory job before the code block begins; if the code block grows beyond a handful of lines, that signals the prose may not have finished its job
Cuts made for concision are only valid when the removed content is genuinely redundant — if it carries information not present elsewhere in the passage, it is not redundant regardless of length

rubric.name: Respects the Reader's Intelligence Without Abandoning Them
rubric.description: Documentation assumes the reader is a competent engineer who is simply unfamiliar with this specific system. It does not over-explain general concepts, but it also does not use minimizing language — 'simply,' 'just,' 'obviously' — that makes a struggling reader feel at fault. Plain, honest language is preferred over theatrical replacements. Difficulty is acknowledged where it exists, and the tone of that acknowledgment sounds like someone who has personally been in the situation, not someone narrating it from outside.

dimensions.label: Does not use minimizing language ('simply,' 'just,' 'obviously,' 'easy') that trivializes genuine complexity
Assumes engineering competence — does not over-explain general concepts unrelated to this specific system
Acknowledges when something is genuinely complex or counterintuitive rather than glossing over it
Tone is collegial — written as one competent engineer explaining to another, not as instructor to student
Replacements for minimizing language are plain and direct — not dramatic or heightened in register
When acknowledging difficulty or confusion, the tone sounds like someone who has personally experienced the situation — not clinically describing it from outside
Conclusions state what the data actually shows rather than offering reassuring verdicts — 'no single node recorded a violation' over 'the limiter is healthy'

writing_type: internal engineering documentation (wiki pages, runbooks, getting-started guides)
user_goals_summary: Documentation that teaches engineers why a system was built the way it was — not just what it does — by surfacing rejected alternatives, naming tradeoffs with specific costs, and placing warnings where they'll be seen before the reader acts. Tone should sound like a competent colleague who has personally hit the failure modes, not a narrator describing them from outside.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every code example gets an orientation comment, every footgun appears before the code it relates to, and every design choice surfaces the rejected alternative. This user's quality bar is set by precision of claims — they will reject language that overstates what the evidence shows ('healthy,' 'behaved as configured') or that sounds narrated from a distance rather than spoken from experience. When offering tone alternatives, avoid theatrical or dramatic register; the user consistently picks the plainest, most direct option. Trade-off assertions without named costs are treated as empty — always specify what would be sacrificed.
